codeReview.summary: The auto-masking module implements pattern-based secret detection and redaction as specified. All 32 tests pass and the workspace builds successfully. However, there are several issues: (1) MaskSecrets trait implementations create a new AutoMasker on every call, defeating stats tracking; (2) some regex patterns are overly broad and could cause false positives; (3) the module is not wired into any tool output pipeline despite the feature description saying it should auto-redact in tool output.

issues.description: MaskSecrets trait implementation for String creates a new AutoMasker::new() on every mask_secrets() call. This means statistics are never accumulated across calls - each invocation gets a fresh masker with empty stats. The stats tracking feature is effectively broken for trait users.
Same issue as above for mask_secrets_with_result() - creates new AutoMasker each call, losing stats.
Same issue for impl MaskSecrets for str.

AWS Secret Key pattern `r"(?i)\b([A-Za-z0-9+/=]{40})\b"` matches ANY 40-character base64 string. This is extremely broad and will cause false positives on any long random-looking string. Consider adding context like 'aws' or 'secret' nearby.
Hex Secret pattern `\b([A-Fa-f0-9]{32,64})\b` will match any 32-64 character hex string including UUIDs, binary encodings, etc. This could mask legitimate data.
API Key pattern `api[_-]?key['\s]*[:=]['\s]*[A-Za-z0-9_\-]{20,}` could produce false positives on text like 'api_key: 123456789012345678901' if the 20+ char value is not actually a secret.
Secret pattern `secret['\s]*[:=]['\s]*[A-Za-z0-9_\-]{16,}` - 16 char minimum may be too low for meaningful false positive prevention.

The module is created and exported but not integrated into the actual tool output pipeline. The feature description says 'Auto-redact secret patterns in tool output' but there's no evidence in the transcript or diff showing this module is wired into tool execution. The module is standalone without any consumer.

sharedStateObservations.observation: AGENTS.md does not document any conventions for secret masking/redaction in tool output. The auto_masking module was created as a standalone utility without any guidance on when or how it should be used. If future workers are expected to integrate this into tool output, the convention should be documented.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: The AGENTS.md covers Rust patterns, error handling, logging, testing, but says nothing about secret handling. The auto_masking module appears to be a new pattern not covered by existing conventions.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The worker used the 'Rust' skill, and skillFeedback indicates procedure was followed. However, the transcript skeleton shows only initial exploration (reading mission.md, features.json, skill files, existing source files) before creating the module. No evidence of consulting any existing patterns or documentation for secret masking approaches.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Transcript shows worker read existing source files (tools.rs, secret_scanning.rs, lib.rs) but found no existing masking implementation to build upon or integrate with.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The worker implemented the masking module from scratch without referencing external documentation. This is reasonable given no prior art existed in the codebase, but worth noting that secret detection via regex has known limitations (context-agnostic, prone to false positives).
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff indicates 'Created new auto_masking.rs module' - no mention of consulting external resources.

summary: The auto-masking feature implements the core functionality correctly - pattern-based secret detection and redaction with [REDACTED] markers. All 32 tests pass. The main concerns are: (1) MaskSecrets trait implementations create new AutoMasker instances per call, breaking stats tracking; (2) several regex patterns are context-agnostic and could cause false positives; (3) the module is not integrated into the tool output pipeline despite the feature description. These are quality/architecture issues rather than correctness failures - the implementation works as a standalone utility but needs refinement before production use.
